1. Introduction
---------------

There is far more microbial cell in human body than [human cell](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/human-cell "Learn more about human cell from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The process of colonization by microorganisms on human body's exposed [surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/surface-property "Learn more about surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as skin, gut, [mouth](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/mouth "Learn more about mouth from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and vagina start immediately after birth. The aggregate of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms occurring on or within human forms [microbiota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/microflora "Learn more about microbiota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and genes collectively that [microbiota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microflora "Learn more about microbiota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") carries forms [microbiome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/microbiome "Learn more about microbiome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The microbiota has beneficial and mutualistic association with their human host and has profound health and physiological impact. Most important and prominent component among human microbiota ecosystem is [gut bacteria](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/intestine-flora "Learn more about gut bacteria from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The number and kind of bacteria are determined by physiological factors such as [intestinal motility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/intestine-motility "Learn more about intestinal motility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), pH, redox state, nutrition, host secretions (e.g., stomach acid, bile, digestive enzymes, and mucus), and the presence of an intact [ileocecal valve](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/ileocecal-valve "Learn more about ileocecal valve from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in various parts of the gut. Aside from intrinsic characteristics, [extrinsic factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cyanocobalamin "Learn more about extrinsic factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as [antibiotic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/antibiotics "Learn more about antibiotic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") usage, disease, stress, aging, poor dietary habits, and lifestyle can all affect [gut microbiota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gut-microbiota "Learn more about gut microbiota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") differences [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772431X22000375#bib0001).

The special projects on human [microbiome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microbiome "Learn more about microbiome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have established that immune environment change supposed to affect the [gut flora](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/gut-flora "Learn more about gut flora from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") which results in [dysbiosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/dysbiosis "Learn more about dysbiosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). “Diseases like cancer, cardiovascular disease, [bowel inflammatory disease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/inflammatory-bowel-disease "Learn more about bowel inflammatory disease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and difficult-to-treat bacterial infections due to [antibiotic resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/antimicrobial-resistance "Learn more about antibiotic resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been linked with dysbiosis” [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772431X22000375#bib0002).

Microorganisms and their metabolites play critical roles in human energy metabolism, nutrition absorption, immunological function, and other vital physiological functions. When [commensalism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/commensalism "Learn more about commensalism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between the host and the microbes is interrupted, a number of human diseases can result.

Many recent investigations have revealed specific strains of live microorganisms that, when administered in sufficient amounts, causes health advantages in the host. These are termed as [probiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/probiotic "Learn more about probiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Scientists have also developed the concept of such food which promotes the growth of beneficial microbiota. These foods are called [prebiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/prebiotic-agent "Learn more about prebiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In the past few years' vast technological improvements and diversity in knowledge have helped in the evolution of [microbiome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/microbiome "Learn more about microbiome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") research, reinforcing our understanding of microbiome and its relationship with [human health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/public-health "Learn more about human health from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Therefore, the objective of this review is to analyze and summarize recent literature reports on the role of the [gut microbiome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gut-microbiome "Learn more about gut microbiome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), on human health and its effect on diseases.

2. Characterization of the gut microbiota: who is in there?
-----------------------------------------------------------

The deviation in healthy [microbiota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/microflora "Learn more about microbiota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be studied if we know which microorganisms resides in human gut in healthy state. According to an estimate microorganisms like bacteria, viruses, fungi, and [protozoa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/protozoa "Learn more about protozoa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that colonizes the [gastrointestinal tract](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gastrointestinal-tract "Learn more about gastrointestinal tract from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") outnumbers [human cell](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/human-cell "Learn more about human cell from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") counting up to 100 trillion [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772431X22000375#bib0003).

Due to vast difference in physiological conditions like pH which differs in different part of the gut, the bacterial and micro-organism's population also vary. 99% of the bacteria of entire [gut microbiome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/gut-microbiome "Learn more about gut microbiome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are [anaerobes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/anaerobe "Learn more about anaerobes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The inner environment of the gut has low oxygen levels which facilitate the expansion of strict anaerobic species of bacteria. In contrast cecum have high densities of [aerobic bacteria](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/aerobic-bacterium "Learn more about aerobic bacteria from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Gut is dominated with six major phyla- [Firmicutes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/firmicutes "Learn more about Firmicutes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [Survival rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/survival-rate "Learn more about Survival rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of microorganisms is extremely low in stomach because of high acidity. By employing a small subunit of [16S rDNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/dna-16s "Learn more about 16S rDNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the first time in the [gastric mucosa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/stomach-mucosa "Learn more about gastric mucosa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); identification of 1056 strains of non-_H._[Pylori](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pylorus "Learn more about Pylori from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), 127 [phylotypes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/phylotype "Learn more about phylotypes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and 5 dominant genera of [Prevotella](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/prevotella "Learn more about Prevotella from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Veillonella](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/veillonella "Learn more about Veillonella from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Rothia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/rothia "Learn more about Rothia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Fusobacterium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/fusobacterium "Learn more about Fusobacterium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_ and _Streptococcus_ was done. Many recent studies used techniques like cloning, sequencing [16S rRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/rna-16s "Learn more about 16S rRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and pyroseqencing is being used and identified that gastric [microbiota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microflora "Learn more about microbiota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") contains primarily non-_H. pylori_ and genera like [Neisseriae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neisseria "Learn more about Neisseriae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_ _[Veillonella](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/veillonella "Learn more about Veillonella from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _, Prevotella,_[Haemophilus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/haemophilus "Learn more about Haemophilus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_[Porphyromonas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/porphyromonas "Learn more about Porphyromonas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_,_ _[Rothia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/rothia "Learn more about Rothia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _, Pasturellaceae Streptococcus,_[Propionibacterium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/propionibacterium "Learn more about Propionibacterium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [Lactobacillus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/lactobacillus "Learn more about Lactobacillus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were dominantly present [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772431X22000375#bib0005).

Gram-positive cocci and bacteria that are rod shaped are predominantly present in [small intestine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/small-intestine "Learn more about small intestine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). However, alkaline environment of the distant part of the small intestine promotes growth of [Enterobacteriacea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/enterobacteriaceae "Learn more about Enterobacteriacea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772431X22000375#bib0004)_._ In Intestinal microbiota dominant phyla are: _[Bacteroidetes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/bacteroidetes "Learn more about Bacteroidetes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _,_ _[Firmicutes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/firmicutes "Learn more about Firmicutes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _,_ _[Actinobacteria](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/actinobacteria "Learn more about Actinobacteria from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _,_ _[Proteobacteria](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/proteobacteria "Learn more about Proteobacteria from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _,_ and _[Verrucomicrobia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/verrucomicrobium "Learn more about Verrucomicrobia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_. Alone _Bacteroidetes_ and _Firmicutes_ constitute 90% of the whole population. _Veillonella, Eschericha, Bacteroids, Claustridium, Lactobacillus, Streptococcus_ are also found in intestinal tract.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The human gut microbiota predominantly consists of Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes, with additional contributions from Actinobacteria, Proteobacteria, Fusobacteria, and Verrucomicrobia, crucial for various metabolic functions.
Microbiome composition varies significantly among individuals and can change due to factors like age, diet, and environmental conditions, reflecting personalized interactions with the host.
Dysbiosis, an imbalance in microbiota, is linked to various health conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases, highlighting the microbiome's role in disease pathogenesis.
